name: french chicken soup
instructions:
BUILD THE FLAVOR BASE: finely chop the leeks AND/OR yellow onion (see notes — often prefer yellow onion here), plus a fraction of the celery and carrots. Sautee in a GENEROUS amount of olive oil until softened — this is the aromatic base. Butter also works but reads slightly richer/more traditionally french (see notes).

SEAR THE CHICKEN: add chicken thighs to the pot and sear well.

DEGLAZE WITH WHITE WINE: pour in a generous splash of white wine and let it reduce briefly. The more wine the better — this is the biggest flavor builder.

ADD BROTH: add stock or msg-rich bouillon cubes dissolved in water. Either works, but msg-rich bouillon is genuinely helpful here — this is a broth, not a sauce.

ADD THE SOUP VEGETABLES: chop the remaining carrots and celery very coarse (much bigger than the base — fork-pickup size). Throw them in the pot along with celery leaves and the bulbs of some scallions. These are soup vegetables now, not flavor base.

HERBS: stir in a generous stack of dried herbs — dried dill, dried tarragon, and dried oregano all belong here, piled on liberally. Fresh tarragon and rosemary also work (fresh tarragon especially — use sparingly, it can overpower). You need some real herb presence here (dried or fresh) or the broth reads flat and muddy. Onion powder and garlic powder are also welcome at this stage.

DUMPLINGS: meanwhile, knead a quick dough of all-purpose flour and MILK (not water — see notes). Cut into small balls and drop into the simmering broth in the last several minutes of cook. See the biscuit-style dumplings recipe for a fully-fleshed-out milk-based dumpling with baking powder.

FINISH: optional fresh-herb garnish — fresh chives, fresh dill, fresh tarragon, or thinly-sliced green onion tops scattered on top before serving. See notes: fresh herbs and green onion tops are attractive garnish but the dried herb stack does the flavor work.
notes:
TARGET DISH SHAPE — BROTH AS MEDIUM, FORK-EATABLE BITS AS THE MEAL: this is not a spoon-up soup like an indian dal or italian grandma soup. It's a salty broth with discrete components — chicken bits, coarse carrots and celery, scallion bulbs, dumplings — that you pull out individually with a fork. You are not expected to drink the broth. The broth is the seasoning medium; the chunks are what you eat. The whole ingredient list flows from this idea.

BIGGEST FLAVOR BUILDERS — WHITE WINE AND FINE HERBS: the white wine is the most important builder — more is better. Fine herbs (tarragon, rosemary, chives) carry the dish; without them the broth reads flat and muddy.

OLIVE OIL IS THE PREFERRED FAT — GENEROUS AMOUNT: this soup is best with olive oil as the fat, used generously. Olive oil keeps the broth clean and broth-shaped — no fatty dairy film on top, no bechamel/roux register to muddy the wine and dried-herb stack. The oil-in-broth is doing real flavor work (compare to the herbs-and-oil register italian broths use). BUTTER IS ALSO VALID (SECONDARY): butter isn't my favorite direction here but it's a real option and adds variation — pushes the dish more traditionally french and slightly richer. Not clashing, just not preferred. And if you want to go the full gravy direction, whisk in a beurre manie at the end to thicken the broth — that turns this into a chicken-and-dumplings-with-gravy dish rather than a broth-meal, which is a real (and different) variation worth having in the toolkit. See rosemary chicken pot pie for the beurre-manie-thickened relative.

ADD FLOUR TO THE CHICKEN AND VEGETABLES BEFORE DEGLAZING — FOR FLAVOR, NOT FOR GRAVY: dust flour onto the seared chicken and softened base vegetables before pouring in the white wine. The flour adds flavor (toasted-flour depth, a little body to the broth). Don't expect a gravy though — the broth-to-flour ratio is way off for that, and gravy isn't the goal anyway. Because you're using olive oil (not butter), the flour + oil combination stays in broth territory rather than reading as roux/bechamel — that's exactly the register you want.

NO HEAVY CREAM (CLASHING): cream is the right move for chicken pot pie filling (gravy), but here we're making a broth to eat things out of, not a sauce to bind things together. Cream muddies the clear broth.

NO PEAS, WHITE BEANS, OR LENTILS (CLASHING): all three are spoon-up foods. Even peas — bite sized but you still scoop them with broth. The high-MSG broth doesn't work with spoon-pickup eating; you'd be drinking the broth alongside the legumes, which is the wrong mouth experience. Peas belong in chicken pot pie (where the gravy is fully consumed inside the pie); white beans and lentils belong in stock-forward soups. They are wrong here.

BOUILLON IS OK HERE — UNUSUAL: most cookbook bean and vegetable soups treat bouillon as an antipattern. This dish is the opposite case: it's a broth-meal, and MSG-rich bouillon gives the salty backbone the dish actually wants. Liquid stock works too but you don't need the gentle layered character — punchy salt and umami are correct.

PILE ON DRIED HERBS — DILL, TARRAGON, OREGANO: this is an unusual case where dried herbs are not just acceptable but optimal — load up as many as you can. Dried dill, dried tarragon, and dried oregano all belong, and they all stack together without fighting. The broth-meal format wants a wall of herb flavor in the salty liquid, and dried herbs (concentrated, not delicate) give you that more reliably than fresh would.

HERBES DE PROVENCE IS THE MOST IMPACTFUL OF THE DRIED HERBS: of the dried-herb stack, herbes de provence carries the most weight. The blend already brings thyme and oregano but the load-bearing components here are the sage and lavender — those are what push the broth from generic-savory-chicken into something specifically french and floral-warm. Neither dried sage nor dried lavender lands cleanly on their own in this dish; the herbes de provence blend gets the ratios right. If you're picking just one dried herb, this is the one to reach for.

FRESH HERBS AND GREEN ONION TOPS ARE GARNISH — DRIED HERBS BUILD FLAVOR: fresh chives, fresh dill, fresh tarragon leaves, and thinly-sliced green onion tops all make an attractive garnish scattered on the finished bowl — they add color and a fresh aromatic pop that dried herbs can't. But they DON'T build flavor into the broth the way dried herbs do. Fresh herbs cooked into the simmer wilt and turn dull; their oils are too delicate for the long broth-cook. The dried stack (dill + tarragon + oregano + herbes de provence) is what makes the broth taste of anything. Treat fresh herbs and green onion tops as visual/aromatic garnish only — a finishing scatter on top after the pour, not an ingredient in the pot.

YELLOW ONIONS CAN REPLACE OR AUGMENT LEEKS — I OFTEN PREFER ONIONS: raw yellow onions in the flavor base — a previously-forbidden move in this recipe — actually work well here. I often prefer the onion character to leeks in this soup; onions add a sharp savory backbone that leeks don't, and the sharpness lifts the broth. You can use yellow onions alongside leeks (both in the finely-chopped sauté base) or replace leeks with yellow onions entirely. Leeks are still fine and traditional; onions are a real and often better option.

ONION POWDER AND GARLIC POWDER WELCOME: alongside (or instead of) the fresh alliums, onion powder and garlic powder both work as dry seasonings in the broth — same logic as the dried-herb stack, this dish wants concentrated dry seasonings driving the broth flavor.

HERB CHOICES — DRIED TARRAGON PREFERRED, PARSLEY/THYME IN A PINCH: dried tarragon is the preferred fine herb for building flavor. Rosemary also works. Parsley and thyme both work in a pinch — they're not ideal (thyme drifts the dish toward rustic european stew, parsley sits in a flatter register than tarragon), but if that's what you have, they're fine. Fresh chives and fresh tarragon leaves belong as garnish on top (see FRESH HERBS above), not as flavor-builders in the simmer.

DUMPLINGS PREFERRED, POTATOES IN A PINCH: flour dumplings are the preferred carb. Potatoes work in a pinch — coarsely chopped and simmered until tender — and give you the fork-eatable starch component without the dumpling-making step. Dumplings are still better here because they take on the broth flavor in a way potatoes don't. Egg noodles and pasta remain wrong (they'd make this a noodle soup, which is a different dish).

WATER + FLOUR DUMPLINGS DON'T WORK — USE MILK: the naive plan of just kneading flour and water into small balls is well-reasoned in theory (simplest possible dumpling) but it doesn't actually work in practice. Even with a heavy hand on baking powder, water + flour dumplings come out too dense — they read as leaden little lumps of raw dough that never lighten. Milk is the way, even though the dough gets stickier and harder to shape by hand. The fat and sugar in the milk are load-bearing for the texture — they give you the tender fluffy chewy dumpling this dish wants. See the biscuit-style dumplings recipe for the full milk + butter + baking powder + salt + sugar version, which is the fully-fleshed-out target.

NO MUDDY GREENS (SPINACH, MIXED GREENS, ARUGULA) — WANT SHARP NOT MUDDY: leafy greens kill the clear sharp broth quality. Scallion bulbs, chives, and the herbs already provide the green notes.

COARSE CHOP THE SOUP VEGETABLES, FINE CHOP THE BASE: the carrots and celery that go in as soup vegetables should be large fork-pickup chunks — they're a meal component. The fraction that goes into the sautee base is finely chopped — that fraction is flavor only. Two roles for the same vegetable; two cuts.

DIFFERENTIATION FROM ROSEMARY CHICKEN POT PIE: same starter vegetables (leeks, celery, carrots), same chicken, same white wine. But pot pie wants cream + peas + potatoes + beurre manie thickener + crust — a contained gravy filling consumed inside pastry. French grandma soup wants broth + bouillon + dumplings + scallions + tarragon — a salty broth with discrete eat-with-a-fork pieces submerged. Different formats, different ingredient logic.
